message:
fix(core+server+cli): pipe-deadlock drain, doctor-orphan-refs rotation ordering, startup port probe, DB agent-visibility probe, git 2.51.x distro-variant detection

Five related hardening fixes, all motivated by operator reports of
"the server hangs / looks stuck" that on investigation traced back
to specific and distinct causes. Packaged together because they all
deal with subprocess/IO/resource hygiene and they each individually
produced the same symptom for users.

================================================================
1) Pipe-deadlock fix in `git_cmd::wait_with_timeout`
================================================================

`crates/mcp-agent-mail-core/src/git_cmd.rs`

Symptom: `am doctor` and orphan-refs sweeps intermittently timed
out on large repos even though git itself finished in <1s. The old
code waited for `child.try_wait()` to return `Some(status)` and
only THEN drained stdout/stderr. On Linux, each pipe buffer is
typically 64KiB — any git invocation that writes more than that to
stdout (e.g. `git for-each-ref --format=... | head`-less) will
block on write once the pipe is full. Our parent, meanwhile, is
looping on `try_wait`, never reading — classic pipe deadlock.

Fix: spawn two reader threads up-front, before entering the wait
loop, so stdout/stderr drain concurrently with `try_wait`. On exit
(success OR timeout OR error), join the reader threads so we don't
leak them; on timeout the drained buffers are attached to the
`Timeout` outcome for diagnostics.

Net effect: the 64KiB ceiling is gone, large git outputs complete
correctly, and the timeout path still works.

================================================================
2) `doctor_orphan_refs`: rotate AFTER all backups are written + skip double-flock
================================================================

`crates/mcp-agent-mail-cli/src/doctor_orphan_refs.rs`

Two bugs that compounded each other:

(a) `rotate_backups` used to run BEFORE `repack_refs`, which meant
    the repack-time packed-refs backup escaped retention for one
    cycle and only got reaped on the NEXT apply. On repos with many
    orphan-refs sweeps per day, this inflated the backup directory
    significantly between sweeps. Moved `rotate_backups` to after
    both the prune-time refs backup AND the repack-time packed-refs
    backup have been written, so retention reaps every backup from
    the current run fairly.

(b) `repack_refs` calls `GitCmd::new(...).run()`, which acquires a
    `RepoFlock` via fcntl. But `scan_one_project` already holds a
    RepoFlock for this repo acquired at the top of the scan.
    POSIX fcntl semantics: a second fcntl(F_SETLK) on the same
    file from the same process succeeds silently and REPLACES the
    previous lock — meaning when the inner `RepoFlock` drops it
    releases the kernel lock even though the outer scan-scope
    `RepoFlock` is still alive. Between drop and the outer lock's
    eventual drop, the repo is unlocked, so a concurrent `am`
    process could squeeze a conflicting operation in.
    Fix: call `.skip_flock()` on the inner `GitCmd` since the outer
    scope already guarantees the lock. In-process mutex still taken
    (cheap, idempotent) so cross-thread serialization is intact.

================================================================
3) Startup port probe: connect() check to distinguish TIME_WAIT from real listener
================================================================

`crates/mcp-agent-mail-server/src/startup_checks.rs`

Symptom: `am serve` sometimes refused to start with "Unknown process
listening on port N" right after `systemctl restart am.service` or
`pkill am`, even when no listener was actually bound. The cause was
TIME_WAIT residue from the just-killed server's accepted peers
keeping the local address in an "AddrInUse" state briefly, confusing
`TcpListener::bind()` into returning EADDRINUSE even though no one
would actually accept() on it.

Fix: after bind() fails with EADDRINUSE and both the health-check
and PID-based identification miss, do a real `connect()` probe to
127.0.0.1:port. If connect refuses immediately with ECONNREFUSED,
nothing is accepting and the bind failure is transient — report
`PortStatus::Free` and let the caller retry with `SO_REUSEADDR`. If
connect succeeds or hangs, the listener is real and we keep the
existing `Unknown process listening` error.

This tightens the port-status classification to three real states:
`Free` / `AgentMailServer` / `Unknown process listening`, where
`Free` now also covers the TIME_WAIT residue case.

================================================================
4) DB: agent-visibility probe after commit + pool-handle fallback
================================================================

`crates/mcp-agent-mail-db/src/queries.rs` (+296 / -143)

When `ensure_agent_present` writes a new agent row and commits, it
then re-selects the row to verify visibility. The previous query
used `ORDER BY id ASC LIMIT 1`, which under some durability states
could return a stale cached row from before the commit. Rewritten
to use a `durability_probe_query` helper that bypasses the read
cache on SQLite and falls through to a pooled-handle verification
(`verify_agent_visible_on_pooled_handle`) if the first probe
returns a known-stale-signal error. The fallback is gated on
`is_agent_visibility_probe_consistency_error(...)` so we only take
the extra round-trip when the specific error class that indicates
a cache stall fires.

Also: reformatted the `reprocess_atc_feature_schema` invocation to
match rustfmt, and updated one `DbError::invalid` callsite for
clarity. No behavior change in those spots.

================================================================
5) Installer: widen known-bad git 2.51.0 detection
================================================================

`install.sh`

`check_git_version_known_bad` used to gate on `case "$git_version"
in 2.51.0)`. That catches the upstream tag exactly but misses every
distribution-rebuild and Git-for-Windows variant of the same
buggy upstream release, all of which carry the same concurrency bug:

  - Git for Windows: `2.51.0.windows.1`, `.2`, `.3`, … (`2.51.0.*`)
  - Ubuntu/Debian:   `2.51.0-1ubuntu1`, `2.51.0-1debian1` (`2.51.0-*`)
  - Build metadata:  `2.51.0+git20260101` (`2.51.0+*`)
  - Prereleases:     `2.51.0.rc1`, `2.51.0-rc1`

Widened the case glob to
  `2.51.0 | 2.51.0.* | 2.51.0-* | 2.51.0+*`
so any version string that starts with `2.51.0` plus a separator
(`.`, `-`, or `+`) is flagged. Comment explains the policy: if it's
a 2.51.0 derivative, the race is presumed present until proven
otherwise. Extensive block comment added above the case so a future
maintainer doesn't narrow this back out thinking distro suffixes
are unaffected.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -2270,8 +2270,18 @@ check_git_version_known_bad() {
   # at runtime via AM_EXTRA_KNOWN_BAD_GIT_JSON (see bead A7); keeping the
   # installer check narrow avoids coupling the installer to a runtime
   # catalog that isn't available until after install.
+  #
+  # The glob patterns below catch:
+  #   - upstream       : "2.51.0"
+  #   - Git for Windows: "2.51.0.windows.1" (and .2, .3, ...) via 2.51.0.*
+  #   - distro suffix  : "2.51.0-1ubuntu1", "2.51.0-1debian1" via 2.51.0-*
+  #   - build metadata : "2.51.0+git20260101" via 2.51.0+*
+  # Any version string that STARTS with "2.51.0" plus a separator ('.',
+  # '-', or '+') is flagged. Bare "2.51.0.rc1" and "2.51.0-rc1" also
+  # match — pre-release or not, if you're shipping a 2.51.0 derivative
+  # the race is presumed present until proven otherwise.
   case "$git_version" in
-    2.51.0)
+    2.51.0 | 2.51.0.* | 2.51.0-* | 2.51.0+*)
       warn "[AM_INSTALL_WARN GIT_2_51_0_KNOWN_BAD]"
       warn ""
       warn "Detected git 2.51.0 ($git_bin) which has a concurrency bug"
